## [18:08 - 21:09]

on Facebook and Instagram, we pretty much have two creative types, video and still image. Pretty simple, video and still image. And one of the biggest questions that I get and we're probably already getting these is Molly, which is better image or video? I got a lot of those in the Q and A's that we got caught up with today. What about Carousel? Which one's better? And this is the very wrong question to be asking guys. This is very similar to trying to figure out which ad copy variation works best or which targeting option works the very best because we hear so much of that language and marketing and talking about split testing and talking about the winner. We always are wondering that in every category that we're working in. Like which is better? But that's absolutely a flawed mindset because as I've talked about the last few weeks, it's not about which one's better. It's about trying to get as many different videos and images in the long run to work and to meet your success metrics as possible because that's what allows you the volume of scale that you're looking for. With boom, we're not trying to figure out what are the best images? What are the best videos? We're trying to pump out as many images and videos that meet the requirements, the success metrics that we're looking for as possible because we know that's truly where scale comes from. So this isn't a game like I saw this on HubSpot video or image which performs better. Yeah, we can all run a simple split test and determine which one worked better. But in the long run, it's really about getting them all to work, especially when we're looking at video versus image because some people are just wired to first read your ad copy and maybe not consume the creative where other folks are gonna consume the creative and maybe only consume the creative or then consume your ad copy. So by using both of these types at all times, that means you are getting the most reach, you are getting the most, you are resonating with more people in the market than if you were just trying to test one versus the other to find the winner because you would be ignoring an entire group of people that are not most likely to engage with that creative type, which is definitely something that Facebook knows when it comes to looking at individual consumers. Like for example, I don't engage much with video for some reason. I'm more of a reader, I skim, but I'm more so engaged with ad copy first and I'm much more likely to engage with a static image. I'm not sure why, it's just how I am, where I know a lot of you guys are much more likely to engage with a video. So just keep this in mind guys, it's not about finding the winner.

## [21:10 - 24:13]

The hope is to create as many of these assets as possible so that we can ultimately scale your business. And here's just a quick example of a project I was working on a few months ago. And the only change, I made no change in terms of audience, I made no change in terms of budget here. This was simply by me adding a different creative type into the mix that was going to appeal to a part of our audience that we might have already been targeting that just weren't interested in the video creative. So instead of just relying on videos as the ones you see here, trailers for what we were working on, you see we were able to add in an image here and this made a really big difference. That made me take action. Can we use that same visual to portray the fact that there is some scarcity here and that people need to take action? ## [1:15:41 - 1:18:43]

She's not just a designer. So if you're interested in chatting with her, interested in hiring her, she also knows the drill, like how this process goes down. Definitely check her out at Brittany, spelled B-R-I-T-N-E-Y, Arkin, A-R-K-I-N, designs.com. She'll be more than happy to work with you guys. And something else that I wanna point out, when you are working with designers, this is a big, big mistake that I see. People hire a designer, and then they send their sales page over, their product page, or their lead magnet page, and they're like, okay, I can't wait to see the videos and images that you create. And we have to remember that, especially most designers, they are not marketers in any way. They are there to create art that looks beautiful and inspires people. They're not coming from a place of what's going to get someone to understand your hook and take the action that you're looking for them to take quickly. And so when you are outsourcing, it's very important that you are pretty specific with this designer on what you're looking for. And you can let them have some leeway, the more that you work with them, or if there's an idea or two that they wanna run with, that they came up with. But especially when Brittany and I just got started working together, it always worked best when I was able to thoroughly explain to her the hook that I was looking to portray, what style I was looking to go with, if I had any other inspiration, any screenshots, whether it's from the News Feed or Facebook ad library that I can give to her, so that she knows what I'm looking for. Because otherwise, you're just gonna get a beautiful video or a beautiful image, which can be great as a marketing asset in other places, but not for your ads. Definitely not for your ads.
